# Future Work

The findings reported in the Experiments chapter raise a number of follow-up questions that fall outside the scope of this paper but represent natural continuations of
the Pytifex research program. The current pipeline establishes that bug-seeded mutation combined with a phased evaluation oracle can expose real implementation gaps
across mypy, pyrefly, zuban, and ty. The system also surfaces several limitations: the curated evaluation set is small relative to the Python typing space, the oracle
cannot resolve every disagreement when the specification itself is ambiguous, and the alpha stage of ty makes its results sensitive to release cadence. The directions
described in this chapter respond directly to those limitations and to questions that the curated dataset opened but could not fully answer.

## Expanding the Evaluation Dataset

Across the six pipeline runs reported in the Experiments chapter, Pytifex produced a total of 106 disagreement-triggering code examples. Including all 106 examples in
the body of this paper would have crowded out the analytical content and made the per-file discussion impossible to read at any reasonable length, so we randomly
selected 25 of those 106 examples and manually verified each verdict for the curated set. This is not a limitation of the pipeline itself, which is capable of
generating many more examples per run than were used in the analysis, but a constraint on what can be presented and discussed in a single paper. The full set of 106
examples is preserved in the project repository and remains available for follow-up analysis.

A natural direction for future work is therefore to run the pipeline for substantially longer windows and to grow the corpus from the current 106 examples to several
thousand. Each additional run produces new seed bugs from the recency-biased GitHub mining stage and new mutated programs from the LLM, so the corpus expands without
manual intervention. A larger corpus would allow finer-grained per-PEP accuracy estimates, would reduce the over-representation of TypedDict examples observed in the
randomly drawn sample of 25, and would make it possible to track the rate at which previously unseen disagreement patterns appear as a function of pipeline runtime.
The exclusion of `loop-newtype-divergence.py` and `mixed-tuple-unpacking-newtype.py` from the curated set also points to a concrete improvement target. Both files were
removed because Hypothesis triggered `ValueError` exceptions from edge case inputs that did not represent genuine type violations. A refined Phase 2 implementation
could distinguish these synthetic failures from real type bugs by inspecting the input that triggered the exception and rejecting cases where the exception originates
from generic Python operations such as tuple unpacking on an empty tuple, rather than from a type-incorrect operation. With that filter in place, files of this kind
could be admitted to the corpus without weakening verdict reliability.

## Reducing the Uncertain Verdict Band Without an LLM

Pytifex currently routes disagreements that survive Phases 0 through 4 either to an UNCERTAIN verdict or, optionally, to a Gemini agent that acts as a final arbiter.
The Experiments chapter notes that two of the 25 curated files received UNCERTAIN verdicts after the full phased cascade, and a larger fraction of the 106-example
corpus reaches the same end state before any LLM call is made. A productive direction for future work is to insert an additional deterministic phase between Phase 4
and the LLM arbiter, with the explicit goal of resolving as many of these UNCERTAIN cases as possible without invoking a model.

The candidate phase would gather every disagreement that the existing four phases mark UNCERTAIN, group them by construct family, and apply targeted analyses
specific to each group. For example, disagreements involving TypedDict totality inheritance would be evaluated against a precise structural rule derived from PEP 589,
disagreements involving `cast()` soundness would be evaluated against PEP 484's explicit "no runtime checking" clause, and disagreements involving Protocol variance
would be evaluated against PEP 544 conformance criteria. Cases that resolve definitively under one of these targeted analyses would receive a CORRECT or INCORRECT
verdict at this new phase. Only the residual cases that no deterministic analysis can settle would then be passed to the LLM arbiter. This routing change would shrink
the LLM's role from a default fallback to a last resort, would reduce the cost and non-determinism of the pipeline, and would also produce a clearer empirical record
of which disagreement categories genuinely require human or model judgment versus which can be settled by careful specification reading.

## Engaging Type Checker Development Teams Directly

The verdicts reported in the Experiments chapter are derived without any input from the development teams that maintain mypy, pyrefly, zuban, and ty. This independence
is intentional and is what allows Pytifex to act as an external auditor of the ecosystem, but it also means that the project has no direct channel for confirming
findings or for understanding why a checker behaves as it does on a given example. A natural next step is to share the corpus and the per-file verdicts with each
maintaining team and to solicit structured feedback on the cases they consider correct, incorrect, or specification-ambiguous.

The feedback loop has value in both directions. From the maintainers' side, the corpus provides a ready supply of minimal reproducers covering recent PEPs, and the
per-file verdicts provide a starting point for triage. From Pytifex's side, the maintainers' responses are ground truth that the phased oracle cannot produce on its
own, particularly for the UNCERTAIN cases where the disagreement reflects a deliberate design choice rather than an implementation bug. A study that incorporates
maintainer feedback into the verdict cascade, perhaps by recording it as an additional phase whose evidence is weighted equally with PEP citations, would produce a
more accurate picture of which disagreements are bugs and which are intended behavior. The same channel could also be used to discuss the targeted oracle extensions
described in the previous section before they are implemented, ensuring that any new automated rule reflects the maintainers' interpretation of the relevant PEP.

## Strengthening the Phased Evaluation Oracle

Two of the 25 curated files received UNCERTAIN verdicts after the full phased cascade. Both involve specification ambiguity rather than evaluation weakness, and the
analysis in the Experiments chapter argues that no purely automated oracle can resolve them without overstepping its evidentiary base. Even so, several construct
categories that produced UNCERTAIN results in earlier pipeline runs could be moved into a definitive verdict band with targeted oracle extensions.

The Phase 0 AST oracle currently covers structural PEP violations such as missing TypedDict keys, unsupported generic subscription, and incompatible callback
signatures. The Oracle rules added during this work for TDICT003 and TDICT004 detect unsafe `NotRequired` access and missing required keys in literal constructions,
and they were directly responsible for resolving several 2-2 splits in the curated set. The same approach can be extended to additional construct families. Variance
violations in user-defined Protocols (PEP 544), redundant `Generic` base classes when PEP 695 syntax is also present, and `Final` redefinition in subclass scopes are
all candidates for AST-level oracle rules because their correctness criteria are stated unambiguously in the relevant PEPs.

Phase 4 (static flow analysis) is the smallest and least developed phase in the current pipeline. It currently performs import availability checks, variance constraint
analysis, and basic narrowing flow inspection. A richer Phase 4 implementation could carry abstract type information through `TypeIs` and `TypeGuard` flow, model the
interaction between `match` patterns and exhaustiveness checks, and validate `ParamSpec` argument forwarding. The shared blind spot that all four checkers exhibited on
`decorator-super-generic-args` is a clear target for this kind of analysis: a Phase 4 pass that traces the wrapped callable's signature through the decorator's
`Callable[P, R]` typing could detect that `super().greet()` is invoked without the arguments declared by the original signature, and could attribute the runtime crash
to the decorator's wrapper body before Phase 1 ever executes the program.

A second extension concerns Phase 2. Hypothesis is currently configured to generate up to 30 examples per callable and to validate return types when `typeguard` is
available. Two refinements would increase its yield. The first is to derive strategies for parametric types such as `TypeVar`, `TypeVarTuple`, and `ParamSpec`, which
the current implementation skips. The second is to integrate the AST oracle's findings into the strategy choice, so that Hypothesis preferentially exercises functions
whose parameter types match the constructs flagged by Phase 0. This coupling would direct property based testing toward the regions of the program where the oracle
already suspects a violation, increasing the likelihood that a generated input triggers a runtime exception attributable to a real type bug rather than to an unrelated
Python operation.

## Resolving Specification Ambiguity Through Upstream Engagement

The two genuinely UNCERTAIN files in the curated set, `unsound-self-cast-divergence` and `typeddict-total-inheritance-divergence`, expose a class of disagreements that
no oracle can resolve while the underlying PEP remains ambiguous. The first case turns on whether `cast()` should be trusted unconditionally as PEP 484 prescribes, or
whether checkers should warn about provably unsound casts. The second case turns on the interaction between `total=False` parents and `total=True` children in
TypedDict inheritance, which PEP 589 does not specify in detail.

A productive direction for future work is to compile the disagreements that fall into this band and submit them to the Python Typing Council and the relevant PEP
authors as concrete specification issues. The Pytifex pipeline already produces the artifacts required for such a submission: a minimal reproducer, the four checker
outputs, the phased oracle's evidence, and a written analysis of why the case is ambiguous. Filing these as typing-council discussions would feed the empirical
findings of differential testing back into the specification process, closing a loop that currently runs only in one direction. The same channel could be used to
propose clarifying language for PEP 589 totality inheritance, for the soundness expectations of `cast()` under PEP 484, and for the runtime behavior of Protocols with
default arguments under PEP 544.

A complementary effort is direct engagement with the four checker repositories. Each Phase 1 INCORRECT verdict in the curated set corresponds to a runtime crash that
the affected checker did not catch. These cases are minimal reproducers of false negatives, and they are exactly the kind of report that maintainers can act on. A
follow-up study could file each verified false negative as an upstream issue and track the time to triage and the time to fix. This would yield two empirical results
of independent interest: a measurement of how Pytifex generated reports compare with organically reported bugs in terms of acceptance rate, and a longitudinal record
of which checkers close which categories of bug fastest.

## Longitudinal Tracking of the Checker Ecosystem

The Experiments chapter measured ty's accuracy at 4.5% over the curated set, but it also noted that ty's conformance score improved across successive releases during
the evaluation period. The same observation applies to pyrefly and zuban, both of which were released during the year preceding this work and continue to receive
frequent updates. A snapshot evaluation captures the state of the ecosystem at one moment, but it cannot distinguish a permanent gap from a transient one.

A longitudinal extension of Pytifex would re-run the curated set against every published release of each checker for some fixed window, recording how verdicts change
as releases land. This would produce a per-checker maturity curve indexed by PEP and construct family, showing where each implementation is improving fastest and
where progress has stalled. The analysis in the Experiments chapter implies that the mypy curve has flattened in regions where two decades of development already
covered the relevant constructs, while ty's curve is steep across most of the test surface. A formal longitudinal study would replace these implications with measured
trajectories.

The same infrastructure would support a regression detection mode. If a release of a previously correct checker begins failing on a curated example, the change can be
flagged automatically and the corresponding upstream commit identified. This would turn the Pytifex curated set into a continuous integration suite that the type
checker community could subscribe to, mirroring the role that LLVM's test suite plays for compiler regressions.

## Reducing Dependence on LLM Generation

The mutation stage of the pipeline relies on Gemini 2.5 Flash to produce candidate programs from seed bugs. This choice was motivated by the success of FuzzGPT
[@deng2024fuzzgpt] and similar bug-seeded approaches, and the 58% per-batch success rate documented in the Methods chapter shows that the LLM is effective at
producing disagreement-triggering programs from a small seed pool. The dependency carries two costs that future work could address.

The first cost is reproducibility. Gemini's output is non-deterministic at the temperatures used during generation, and the seed pool itself shifts across runs because
the GitHub mining stage uses recency biased sampling. A future iteration of Pytifex could record every generation prompt and every model response in a local cache,
allowing exact replay of any pipeline run. Combined with a pinned model version, this would make the generation stage fully reproducible without sacrificing the
benefit of recency biased seed selection.

The second cost is monetary. Gemini 2.5 Flash is a paid model, which means that researchers and developers who wish to run Pytifex must either obtain API credits or
limit themselves to the small free tier that Google provides. This barrier is at odds with the project's goal of being a freely available auditing tool for the Python
typing ecosystem. A future iteration of Pytifex should therefore migrate the mutation stage onto a free or fully open model, such as a locally hosted Llama variant or
a comparable open-weight model that can be downloaded and run without metered API access. The mutation prompts described in the Methods chapter are not specific to
Gemini and should transfer to any sufficiently capable instruction-tuned model with minimal adjustment. A study that compares per-batch disagreement yield across a
free model and Gemini 2.5 Flash on the same seed pool would establish whether the move sacrifices any pipeline effectiveness, and would make Pytifex usable by anyone
without payment.

## Extending the Pipeline as the Checker Set Grows

Pytifex currently evaluates four checkers and mines from five repositories, including pyright as a seed source even though pyright is not part of the differential
testing stage. The Cambrian explosion of new Python type checkers described in the Introduction has not slowed: pyrefly, zuban, and ty all appeared within roughly a
year of one another, and the trend across recent years suggests that further checkers are likely to enter the ecosystem on a similar cadence. A future direction for
Pytifex is therefore to extend the differential testing stage as new checkers arise, rather than as a single up-front engineering task.

The `run_checker_on_code` function in the Methods chapter already accommodates additional commands, and the disagreement filter operates on whatever set of checker
statuses is supplied to it. Adding a new checker to the bank therefore requires registering its invocation command, mapping its exit status to the `ok` or `error`
convention, and re-running the curated examples against it. Pyright is the most immediate candidate because it already serves as a seed source, and any further
checker that emerges from the same trend can be added on the same path. Treating the checker bank as an open-ended set rather than a fixed quartet keeps Pytifex
aligned with the ecosystem it audits, and ensures that the maturity gradient reported in the Experiments chapter remains current as new entrants reshape it.

## Addressing the Theoretical Barrier

The Experiments chapter invokes Rice's Theorem to explain why some disagreements admit no automated resolution. This is a real boundary, but it is not absolute. For
constructs whose semantics can be expressed in a decidable fragment of type theory, model checking and formal verification techniques can produce definitive verdicts
where heuristic oracles cannot. A future direction is to integrate a small theorem proving backend into Phase 4 for the subset of constructs where the relevant PEP
admits a precise formal specification. PEP 484 generic variance, PEP 544 protocol conformance, and PEP 589 TypedDict structural compatibility are candidates because
each has a reasonably crisp set-theoretic interpretation. A backend based on Z3 or a similar SMT solver could discharge proof obligations for these constructs and
move them out of the UNCERTAIN band entirely. The cases that remain undecidable would then be those that genuinely depend on dynamic Python features, which is the
correct location of the boundary.

## Summary

The Pytifex pipeline as described in this paper is a working differential testing agent that produces verifiable false negative reports for four Python type checkers.
The directions outlined above respond to the limitations identified in the Experiments and Methods chapters and convert each of them into a concrete research
extension. Growing the corpus beyond the 106 examples produced for this paper, inserting a deterministic phase that resolves UNCERTAIN cases before any LLM call,
incorporating direct feedback from the four checker development teams, strengthening the phased oracle, engaging upstream with verified findings, tracking the
ecosystem longitudinally, migrating the mutation stage to a free model, extending the checker bank as new entrants arrive, and integrating formal techniques where the
specification permits, together describe a research program that takes Pytifex from a snapshot evaluation of the current Python typing landscape to a continuous
instrument for measuring it. The findings reported in this paper indicate that such an instrument is needed: the maturity gap between the established and emerging
checkers is large, the specification itself is imperfect, and the rate at which new typing PEPs land continues to outpace the rate at which existing checkers
stabilise. Each of the directions described here is a step toward closing that gap.
